course_description:
Understanding of geomechanical behaviour of orebody and host rock is of critical importance to achieve safe and efficient mining operations and has become an integral part of a mine design and planning. As mines are getting deeper, the ground in situ stress and water pressure are also increasing, which requires more in-depth understanding of rock mass to address this emerging grant challenge in deep mining. This course provides students with a fundamental knowledge of rockmass properties, and with a practical understanding of the applications of geotechnical engineering principles in mining from the perspective of planning, design, and operations. This will enable students to understand and apply fundamental concepts and design methodologies to design safe excavations in both surface and underground mines.

This is a 2-unit course providing students with fundamental knowledge of geomechanics and then applying the relevant theories to conduct geotechnical investigations, assessment, and safety design of mining excavations.

The course comprises three parts:

(i) fundamentals of mining geomechanics, such as the mechanical properties and strength of intact rock and rock mass, and rock mass classification systems;

(ii) geotechnical challenges associated with open-pit mining, e.g., slope stability, monitoring and instrumentation, and instability forecast; and

(iii) geotechnical challenges related to underground mining operations (both hard rock and soft rock), such as rock reinforcement/support techniques, excavation stability, mine seismicity, caving mechanics, pillar design, and subsidence.

course_aims: This course equips students with fundamental knowledge of intact rock and rockmass properties, applying geotechnical engineering principles from the perspective of mining planning, design, and operations. Students then apply these fundamentals and testing methodologies to proficiently design safe excavations and conduct stability assessment for both surface and underground mining operations.

learning_outcomes.description: Evaluate rock competence through relevant lab testing and classification systems to inform mining geotechnical designs
Assess slope stability and failure modes by integrating stereographic projection, deterministic and probabilistic approaches using software tools.
Analyse the stress distribution in underground excavations to evaluate the stability and ground support options.
Assess caveability in block cave mining and stability in longwall mining.
Propose a technical management plan to address the core geotechnical risks associated with mining methods

assessment_details.title: Rock mechanical properties testing
assessment_details.due_date: 13/03/2026 4:00 pm The lab prac is scheduled for Week 2.
assessment_details.task_description: The prac will be held at lab 50-S107. Instruction on lab access will be provided when issuing the assessment. The class will be divided into two groups of your choice due to space constraints in the lab. Please enrol in your preferred group as soon as possible. You only need to attend your chosen group. Attending the lab prac is not compulsory, but strongly encouraged. For those who are unable to attend the practical session, the laboratory data will be made available on Blackboard to analyze and complete their reports. The submission due date will remain the same for everyone unless your extension request is approved by the university. Other important information includes: Please read the practical handout prior to your arrival. Students must wear safety shoes and long pants. Gloves and safety glasses will be provided on the day. It is a condition of entry to practical classes that you have completed the Student Safety Declaration Form provided by a member of the teaching team before the class. When you arrive at the practical venue, it is also compulsory to sign in, and sign out as you leave the laboratory. Five types of rock testing will be carried out during the prac, which runs for 2 hours or slightly over. There will be two separate groups, and you can sign up for your preferred group via Blackboard. Please refer to Blackboard for a detailed marking criteria. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

Submit via TurnItIn on Blackboard.

The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Slope stability analysis- field mapping
assessment_details.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Practical/ Demonstration, Project
assessment_details.task_description: This is an individual assignment. The class will be split into two groups, which you can enrol via Blackboard. Field mapping practical will be conducted at the base of the Kangaroo Point cliffs. A map with the exact location and the details of requirements will be uploaded to Blackboard. Attending the field mapping is not compulsory, but strongly encouraged. When attending, you are required to wear steel-capped safety boots, long sleeves and long pants. You will not be admitted to the practical class if you fail to wear the prescribed personal protective equipment . You are also advised to bring a clipboard, pencils and rubber, sunscreen and a hat. Please refer to Blackboard for a detailed marking criteria. For those unable to attend, please try to obtain the data from a classmate or contact Zhongwei for assistance. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.task_description: Five individual online quizzes, each worth 2%, will be held in Weeks 3, 4, 7, 8, and 10. Students will have a one-week window to access and complete each quiz via Blackboard Ultra. The relevant quiz links will be released on Blackboard closer to the scheduled dates. Please refer to Blackboard for detailed marking criteria. Each quiz consists of approximately five questions and has a time limit of 15 minutes. Students are permitted two attempts for each quiz. assessment_details.title: Final Report and Oral Defence
assessment_details.due_date: Final Report - 4:00 PM 25/05/2026 Oral Defence Block - 8/06/2026 - 10/06/2026
assessment_details.task_description: This individual assessment consists of two integrated components: a technical report and an online oral defence. The report assesses students’ technical understanding of the topics covered in the course, while the oral defence further evaluates the learning outcomes demonstrated in the report. To pass the course, students must pass the oral defence. Detailed guidelines for both the report and the oral defence will be provided in due course. Please refer to Blackboard for detailed marking criteria. The final report is due at 4:00 pm on 25 May 2026. Oral defence will be scheduled during the first week of the examination period across three dates (9:00 am–5:00 pm, 8–10 June 2026). Students will be able to select a time slot that best suits their availability. Each student will be allocated 10 minutes to respond to questions related to their report, and the oral defence will be assessed by at least two markers. This assessment task evaluates students' abilities, skills and knowledge without the aid of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT). Students are advised that assistance from other persons, resources or the use of AI or MT technologies to develop responses is strictly prohibited and may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Final report submit via TurnItIn on Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
As the Oral Defence component is timed-assessment, Student Access Plans (SAPs) and discretionary requests are not suitable supporting documentation. Students must provide evidence that demonstrates their inability to attend the assessment at the scheduled time.
Students submitting an extension must indicate whether the extension request is for the Final Report or Oral Defence component.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
100% late penalty applies to the Oral Defence Block. Consistent with industry practice around presentations to clients/industry partners, no late submissions will be accepted. This has been approved by the Associate Dean (Academic).
